summary: The SA node is the primary pacemaker of the heart with an intrinsic firing rate of 70-80 beats per minute.
The AV node is the primary pacemaker with a firing rate of 100 beats per minute.
The Bundle of His is the primary pacemaker with a firing rate of 40 beats per minute.
Cardiac output equals stroke volume multiplied by heart rate, normally about 5 litres per minute.
Cardiac output equals blood pressure divided by peripheral resistance.
Normal resting cardiac output is approximately 10 litres per minute.
The Frank-Starling law states that increased venous return (preload) increases the force of cardiac contraction.
The Frank-Starling law states that increased afterload increases the heart rate.
The Frank-Starling law describes the relationship between heart rate and blood pressure.
The first heart sound (S1) is produced by closure of the atrioventricular (mitral and tricuspid) valves.
The first heart sound (S1) is produced by closure of the semilunar (aortic and pulmonary) valves.
The first heart sound (S1) is produced by opening of the mitral valve.
The long refractory period of cardiac muscle prevents tetanus, ensuring the heart relaxes between contractions.
The short refractory period of cardiac muscle allows summation of contractions.
Cardiac muscle can undergo tetanic contraction like skeletal muscle.
Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch mediate short-term blood pressure regulation.
Chemoreceptors in the carotid body mediate short-term blood pressure regulation.
The renin-angiotensin system is the primary mechanism for short-term blood pressure regulation.
The Fick principle calculates cardiac output as oxygen consumption divided by arteriovenous oxygen difference.
The Fick principle calculates cardiac output as blood pressure divided by heart rate.
The Fick principle measures cardiac contractility using echocardiography.
